speaker: interviewee
text: You mean before I go to sleep, right? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So to take us back to where we were talking about, you said you only thought about being Jewish. You didn't think about the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wondered about it. It was not something-- I wondered about it. You know, like you say, now that I know I'm Jewish, do I feel different? Do I look different? Or what does it mean being Jewish? Except I can't play the lead in a movie, what has that to do with anything?

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, my mother kept saying, you know, they only mean the German Jews. And I kept saying, I'm not a German Jew. I'm Austrian. So why can't I do my movie? Has nothing to do with me. You keep saying it has nothing to do with us here, then why can't I do my movie? It was all that I was concerned about. And you know, make sense of Jewish, you can't play the lead in a movie. Didn't make sense at all. Ashamed? How can I be ashamed of something I don't even understand? Did you then then seek out any friends or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two friends, I found out at that time, that they were Jewish-- one of them assimilated, like we, and the other one, I found out later, she must have been Orthodox. First of all, she used to come to our house and eat all our ham. And I didn't understand why she would eat our ham. And then I went once to her house. And I saw her mother wore a, a, a wig. And I came home and I said to my mother, oh, my gosh, poor Mrs. Birnbaum. She must've been very sick. She doesn't have any hair. She has to wear a wig.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother started laughing. She said, well, don't you know anything about Jews and Jews? And she told me that they were Orthodox Jews. And some of the old laws say that she needed to wear a wig because she wasn't supposed to show her hair or something like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, I already had religion classes in school, except I didn't go. I went only twice. And for the rest of the time, I played football. It wasn't really compulsory. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: About your Orthodox friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, yes, yes. Anyway, my mother said-- yes, I had religious classes at that time, but I didn't really go because we had to learn to read Hebrew. Not for me-- I didn't like this at all. I liked, I liked the Bible. You know, for me, it was like fairy tales-- very interesting. But not that reading from left to right, whatever it is-- I just wasn't interested in it. So I never found out about Orthodox and assimilated and-- only through her. And then my mother explained to me that there was a great difference and that probably that's the reason that Stella-- Birnbaum was her name-- Stella Birnbaum-- that she came to the house to eat our ham. Because she was not allowed to have ham. And I always thought it was because they were stingy and they didn't want to spend the money on the ham. That's why. So this was the only way I knew she was Jewish and, and observed probably all the-- but we never talked religion. You know, we never talked about where, what, or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you member of any organizations at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a, as a child, I belonged to something which the closest I can come is kind of the Girl Scouts here, except it wasn't. You know, it was a bit rougher-- rougher in the sense of we did a lot of rough outings. You know, we really were roughing it, you know, just being outside in tents overnight and things like this. And then were a lot of sports competition going on. That I remember. It was not a political organization or anything like that. A matter of fact, I was an actress, I remember my, my mother had to sign something that we were not allowed to join any political organization at all. My sister-- my twin-- got into political things to such a degree that my mother had to send her away to England because that was strictly forbidden. And she brought home strange characters-- very, very weird characters. I think they were the first hippies, definitely. And they always-- she always gave them all our food which was in the pantry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when she sent your sister--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about when I was 15, 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what were the first signs of danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, none at all, for me, none at all-- They were not in Vienna, either. Again, none again I knew about. I just-- there was nothing. Life went on normal. I had my studies. I had my work. I had my theatre. And in Vienna itself, there was no such thing you cannot appear here if you're Jewish. Or let me see whether you are Jewish or not. It just wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did life change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Life didn't change for me in Vienna at all, not at all. It just-- I went away. I left because of a contract, but not because it changed in Vienna. And if there were any rumors, they were political rumors-- not, not as far as Nazis are concerned, but inner politics about this mayor and this mayor-- I mean, you know, government things, strictly government things.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left Vienna. I had a contract as star of a show to Switzerland to tour for six weeks. And I left Vienna exactly the day the Nazis much into Austria. I crossed the border into Switzerland the day the Nazis marched in. It had nothing to do with foresight. I had not a clue, of course not. Except I never came back. I left for six weeks and came back for the first time only because my mother was still alive, 26 years later. So that's, that's when it started to be aware. And, again, I wasn't really aware or did understand. I did not understand. I celebrated success. I had fantastic critics. I had fantastic success. And it's all I celebrated, except I should've known. In my passport at that time, it was customary to have your religion. It was called Mosaic Jewish. I don't think that exists anymore. But you had your religion. And my indicator should have been short-term visas-- entered, left, entered, left, entered, left. I had become a gypsy. I went from one country to the other, wherever they would let me in, work, stay a while, go onto the next, work. I spent a lot of time in crummy little offices in police stations-- not because I was arrested, but asking for a visa, permission to work, permission to stay another week, another week, another week. And, actually, for two years, I was always a bit ahead of the Nazis. Again, had nothing to do with foresight. It just happened that way. I remember, I had a contract in Czechoslovakia, had laryngitis, had to cancel. So I was in another country just that the Germans marched into Czechoslovakia. When Poland was crushed, I went from one country to the other, always ahead of the Nazis. I wasn't running away from them. It just happened that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that point, you were just hearing rumors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew. But, you know, if somebody says to you, let's take a totally strange, foreign word which you have never heard before, try to make sense of it, first of all, why? What? You can't really believe it. You can't believe it. You haven't seen a Nazi. You haven't seen one of those German crazy guys. So what-- it's hard-- you just know that it becomes harder and harder to get into a country and to stay in a country and to work-- to get a work permit. Everything was temporary. Your life had become temporary-- perhaps a day, perhaps a week, perhaps a month. And you had become a gypsy, that's all. You had become a gypsy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the contact that you had with your, your mother and your sister at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could still, still write. Didn't mean that all the letters arrived. And we could also still call long distance if and when you could get through. That was all maybes. It was possible, but it was a lot of maybes. And I even could send money to mutti. And whatever I have left, for instance, even today on critiques or pictures from then are what I had sent to mama. And she kept it and able to take it to other countries and so on. But I could for a while. Again, it wasn't that I knew. And we tried to get people out of Vienna. We tried to get people out, help them-- my producer-- to get out of a fake contract. And for the first about nine months, it was not so difficult. All you had to do was bribe an Austrian official and show that you have a visa in another country. The place then to be was Shanghai. It was the only place where they let you in and let you stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I mean, even where they still played Viennese music all over the place-- they played it everywhere in every country in the world-- no longer in Vienna. They had to play marches-- the German marches. And your mother stayed in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mutti was in Vienna. And my older sister, who had married meanwhile, also was in Vienna-- stayed with my mother in the same apartment. So they were together. So at least she wasn't alone and had also help, you know, financially. And my twin was in England-- was in London, which was a blessing, you know, really. She didn't think so at that time, but-- and that was really all we had on the immediate family there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother reported to you what was happening when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: She couldn't. You see, you had to write-- a matter of fact, you already had to-- I remember I wanted to send her a critique of one of the shows I was in. And the Swiss critic had written-- had made some particular innuendos, remarks about what was going on in Austria. And before I could send this, I had to cut it off. I wouldn't have dared to send her these things. You know, it's-- one wonders will Vienna ever sing again. You know, so all of the political inferences which were made by other countries, I could not send to her. So this was all-- or in the letters. I mean, you had to be careful. You didn't know whether it was censored or not. You certainly wouldn't write openly what is going on-- are you suffering or are they beating you or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned that, for two years--

speaker: interviewee
text: For two years-- for two years, I was always really one step ahead. And then finally, it came to it that actually, I mean, I had-- my mother is-- my father was all Austrian, Viennese. And, and my mother is Hungarian, from Budapest. And there's a whole string of, of relatives there. A matter of fact, I always thought all of Budapest was related to her. She was an only child, but everybody was uncle, aunt, whatever. And there was a family very, very close to her-- second cousin of mine. And with them, we were always in contact. And I used to go to Budapest whenever I could to get away from it. They didn't understand anything at all over there. They had not a clue to what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that my cousin didn't. And I didn't. So between the two of us, none of us understood what was going on. We all thought, you know, really, this is-- whatever it is, it's going to blow over. Nobody expected to make this what it had become. It didn't blow over. There's a nut. There's a little crazy guy running around, you know? Tell me about that crazy guy running around.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the crazy guy's Mr. Adolf, you know? But the thing is that I remember. I was in Belgium. I was in Belgium working. And whoever you talked to, nobody took it serious and nobody believed. Everything was blown out of proportion. And at that time, we didn't blow anything out of proportion. It was under. I mean, I just kept on singing. I didn't understand anything. I just knew then my mother was in Holland. I had gotten her out to Holland. I had gotten a contract to Holland, to, to The Hague. And I got permission to get her out to visit her daughter. She knew then she wouldn't return to Vienna. And that was very rough on her. A matter of fact, most Viennese suffered from a great deal of homesickness. I think I'm the only one who never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she take with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She couldn't take anything. She left everything with the concierge. She do-- she handed over the keys. The fur-- nothing, she couldn't take anything. It was just a visitor visa. She was not-- you know, she was not immigrating. She only got permission to visit her daughter. This is what it was. But she knew that she would not return anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- when did you see your first Nazi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not for a very-- I wasn't looking for-- I wasn't really looking for them. Not for a long time, I must say. I then-- let me see, i was in Belgium. And my mother was in Holland with my older sister and her husband. And I thought, I'd best get to where my family is. And the producer-- our original producer, anyway-- he had a feeling that Belgium wouldn't be safe very long, either. And he thought that we should get out of Belgium and should get to Holland also. I don't know why or who had applied for immigration visas to America. And that was in Holland. So I figured, you know, I need to go to Holland if that is supposed to come through one day. And I remember that I literally took a taxi from Belgium and told the driver, take me to another country, and went to Holland. It was the only country where you still could get in-- you know, just into the country. All the other countries, especially Switzerland, was closed. You couldn't get into Switzerland anymore. They had closed the borders. And the only thing they kept open was the banks for the war promoters. They were all open, all open and for the money. But nobody could get in. In other words, I had run out of countries to run to. So I went to Holland, to The Hague back. And, at that time, my mother had already moved to the seashore of a place called Scheveningen-- not easy to pronounce. And I got my old apartment back. And that was only few days before the invasion of Holland. Because Belgium got-- a matter of fact, when I got to, to Holland, I missed the Stormtroopers by two days. Or, rather, they missed me. So then I was in Holland and got my apartment back. And on May 10, 1940, the invasion of Holland took place-- lasted five days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in a very pretty residential square. And I remember, on the corner of that, there was a military [? kasern. ?] And I'd never seen anybody in there before. And just a couple of days before the invasion, I saw some soldiers there. But they were walking around with a cigarette in their mouths and with open things, you know. They couldn't-- no idea. You have to realize Holland didn't have a war for hundred years. And also, anti-Semitism has never existed in Holland. Because the, the Dutch Jews, shoemakers, and bankers, and, and little tailors, or selling eel on the street, and industrialists. So is, you know, there is both. When the invasion took place, it was very weird. It is like somebody shows you a lot of slides. And they're faulty-- and very rapidly, very rapidly. The radio would say, we don't have anything to worry about and the bombs already dropped. And everything is under control. And people are running back and forth and into each other. And then, then we heard the queen had fled. I mean, it was total commotion, total-- nobody knew what to do. They didn't have an army. And they thought that if they open all the dams, the Germans can't march in. Well, in a very, very peculiar, sinister way, they were right. Because they didn't march. When they got tired of playing games, this-- the Germans summoned 500 bombers, bombed Rotterdam, flattening it completely, and then, you know, moved in with the invasion-- nothing. They fought for five days. And, even so, they didn't stand a prayer. There was a group of incredible brave men who fought and fought and fought, knowing it is suicide. But they fought in spite of it. And of course, and then they were there. They were everywhere. And something very strange happened with that whole country. It became so quiet. You didn't see anyone on the street. You didn't see anyone anywhere. All the windows were shut. The doors were shut. It was like the whole country had stopped breathing. And it was like it was in mourning. It was already in mourning of what was yet to come. I lived very close with a tenor and his lady friend and a comedian. We were very close together. And after the first five days and you're kind of, you know-- got back a little bit you to reality. You decided something has to happen. You didn't know what would-- what would happen. We had nothing to compare it with. We didn't have another country where they said, this is, this is going to happen now to the Jews or to this-- we didn't know. Nobody knew. All we knew is that we still have the power to decide something for ourselves. And we-- this small group, the friends, the tenor, and his lady friend, and the comedian, and I-- decided we want to try to get to England, which was not crazy. Because from the coast, there are a lot of little shipping boats. And you can see, practically, on a clear day, see Dover.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we wanted to get to England, which was never farther away or never closer. And this girlfriend of this tenor decided not to go at the last moment. The comedian decided not to go. So tenor and I went alone few days after the invasion had stopped. And I still hadn't seen a Nazi, not one single German-- never mind Nazi-- no German at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the walk from The Hague to that coast is about-- if you walk-- I would say about an hour. I can't recall exactly. And that morning on May 20-- that's it, May 20-- we left. There was nobody on the streets-- still, 10 days after the invasion, nobody on the streets. It was very, very early. And we didn't see any friends. We didn't see any enemies, no one. We came to the coast. And the whole coast-- one of the busiest resorts in Europe, used to be-- I mean, buzzing, buzzing every year, thousands of people and hotels and restaurants and coffee houses and I mean, full, the beaches full-- like an empty movie set. There was nothing there. Nobody was there. Nothing was there. The places were all closed. It was eerie. It was absolutely eerie. We spent-- waited, waited for a fishermen, for a boat, for something all day long. It was awful, absolutely awful. And then toward evening, there was a man coming toward us. It was a, a, a Dutch guy-- a fisherman. And we wanted him to take us-- him-- us across. And he said, no more. What had happened is-- when, I have no idea-- he told us that his brother was blown up the day before with another friend of his bringing a whole boat full of Jewish people across. Mines were there. The whole North Sea was full of mines. I had no idea when they were put there. They weren't there when they first started. I know they weren't there, because they had a lot of-- a lot, perhaps a handful, I don't know how many-- got across. But now his brother was dead. He had no boat. The people were dead. And he had just come from his wife-- from his brother's wife, who had left five children, and to tell her that her husband had died. And she didn't understand anything. She thought he was drunk. I mean. she was making-- he was making up a story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember thinking, you know, the war is over for them. For us, it just had begun. And we didn't know anything. We didn't know about mines. We didn't know about Jews. We didn't know anything. He went back-- Kurt, he went back to his girlfriend, at least for a while. And I moved in with my family. And short afterwards, the first sign of being Germans and Nazis there started [? every ?] [? creation-- ?] signs everywhere. Who first? Jews first. We had to gather at the little round square. Where were we sent to? Nobody knew. When? A couple of hours. What can we take? One suitcase. My non-Jewish brother-in-law, the husband of my oldest sister, had just come from The Hague to bring all my clothes, which I now could leave behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and I were sent to a place called Enschede, not a place to be at any time. It's E-N-S-C-H-E-D-E. It's very close to the German border, much too close for comfort. You know, really, it's-- you may as well-- you can look over and you're in Germany. And my sister with her husband was sent, which we didn't know for many, many, many months where they were. And after a few days in this place, which was-- you had the feeling everybody is a zombie, asleep. I don't know whether they were afraid because they were so close to the German border or what it was or whether they hadn't gotten over the invasion yet. I can't tell you, but it was very weird. And I had found the committee-- Jewish committee whatever-- little man there. And I told him I couldn't stay here. I needed to be where I could work-- you know, no money, no nothing. And he gave me permission to go to a place called Utrecht-- U-T-R-E-C-H. It's in the south of Holland. It's known for being very cultural, for a very, very good university and a beautiful theatre, which were both closed now. And, and my mother, unfortunately, had to stay in this awful place, Enschede. I remember, much later, thinking, if I told him I was an actress, I needed to go somewhere else, if I would have thought of, I would have told him that mama was an actress, too. So she could have gotten out with me. It took many months until I got her out. Then I was in Utrecht, which, as I said, I knew from my tours with the-- with the theatre in The Hague. I knew every place in, in Holland. And what I did there was all the people who had been evacuated-- they were Jewish-Dutch families took them in. And the Jewish committee would give us pocket money, which was very nice. Jews-- you know, Dutch Jews would give to them and they would give to us. And there was a Jewish committee that was in a small, old house, two stories. And every Jew who had been evacuated was there, day and night, with nothing else to do. And that was the rumor factory. You could-- whatever you wanted to hear on rumors was there. My brother told me. My mother told me. I know where this one is. We can go home soon. Everything was there. It was a, it was a crazy existence. Nothing was good. Nothing was bad. You were alive. You ate. You slept. But it was kind of a nothing, absolutely nothing. You know, it was-- and interestingly, there you could see how totally naive not I, not Austria, not the other countries, everybody was. These Dutch people, for instance, where I was, was two teachers-- a young couple with a baby, teachers-- very intelligent, spoke, like most Dutch, several languages. And they kept living their life as though nothing had happened. There was no boogie man in the backyard. Everything was fine-- the exactly same routine they have had all along. Nothing changed. The Germans were already in Paris. They were all over the place. You heard only Sieg Heil, Sieg Heil over the Dutch airwaves. You saw them everywhere, but not a single French plane, not a single English plane, certainly not an American plane. We kept waiting for them to come. And nobody came. But the routine of the Dutch went on. Again, it can't happen here. They will not do anything to us. We've been always friendly to the Germany, Netherlands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I?

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't understand them. I remember that my mother said, they mean them, over there. They don't mean us. And now the Dutch were saying, they mean all-- the Poles, the Czech, the Austrians-- they don't mean us. Why us? First of all, as I said, there is no a-- there was no anti-Semitism there. This also makes a great-- the Dutch Jews didn't start riot-- the Dutch people didn't start rioting and say, dirty Jew. Didn't happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Utrecht, I was a few months until the producer I had originally left Vienna with got permission for me to come to Amsterdam-- the mission was given from the Dutch police president in Amsterdam -- to work in a theatre. He had the work papers and the papers that I could stay there. That's when I came to Amsterdam, which was the most fascinating of all. Because there, nobody knew what was going on. I mean, this was really life as usual. It was full of German immigrants. And they all did fabulously well, because they had already left-- most of them-- in '33. We had talked now already about '40-- 1940, late 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had their textile industries. They had the fur industries. They had their businesses as usual and were doing very, very well. What was the name of the theatre that you worked in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at first I worked at the Beatrix Theatre-- like the name Beatrix, B-E-A-T-R-I-X, like one-- you know, the queen's, of the queen's daughters. And then, later on, I changed to the same-- it was in the same street, just a few houses away was another theatre, which was the Hollandsche Schouwburg. Hollandsche Schouwburg-- Hollandsche is Dutch, Schouwburg is the Dutch name for theatre.
